title: HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings
concept: Generating a hypothetical answer first, then embedding that answer to search, since answers match documents better than questions do.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we explored LLM-based query rewriting. What is its main purpose?

options.text: Storing database documents in multiple languages.
Using an LLM to reformulate a messy user query into a search-optimized version.
Compressing vectors to save storage space.

exercises.explain: In the prior lesson, we learned that LLM-based query rewriting takes raw user queries and reformulates them (adding synonyms, correcting typos, and expanding terms) to make them easier for retrieval systems to match.

cards.title: The Question-Answer Gap
cards.body: When you search a database, you usually compare a short, curious question to a long, informative document. But vector search works much better when it compares similar formats—like matching an answer to another answer.

cards.title: What is HyDE?
cards.body: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) is a technique where you use an LLM to generate a fake, hypothetical answer to a user's question, and then use the embedding of that fake answer to search your database.

cards.title: The Mock Recipe Trick
cards.body: Imagine looking for a recipe book. Instead of searching 'how to make cookies', you write a fake recipe card of what the page might look like, and then search the library for books containing text that resembles your mock recipe card.

cards.title: HyDE in Action
cards.body: Instead of searching the database with the question 'What causes northern lights?', an LLM first writes a paragraph describing solar winds. This generated paragraph is then embedded and used to find scientific papers with similar phrasing.

cards.title: The Accuracy Myth
cards.body: You might think the hypothetical answer needs to be 100% factually correct. In reality, even if the LLM hallucinatingly invents some details, the structural style and terminology are close enough to guide the vector search to the correct real document.

cards.title: Standard vs. HyDE Search
cards.body: Comparing what gets embedded to search the vector database. Standard search sends a question, while HyDE sends a fully-formed answer style.

left.text: Embeds: 'How do fish breathe underwater?' (Question format)

right.text: Embeds: 'Fish extract dissolved oxygen from water using their gills...' (Answer format)

exercises.prompt: What makes HyDE different from standard query embedding?

options.text: It searches the vector database directly using the user's raw question.
It translates the query into database SQL commands.
It embeds a generated hypothetical answer instead of the raw question.
It deletes the original query and forces the user to write their own document.

exercises.explain: HyDE generates a hypothetical document (fake answer) using an LLM first, and then converts that generated text into an embedding to run the vector database query.

exercises.prompt: True or False: HyDE does not require an LLM to generate any text; it only uses standard embedding models.
exercises.explain: HyDE relies on an LLM to generate the hypothetical document before the embedding step. If we bypass the LLM, we are just doing standard query embedding.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the core principle behind HyDE's retrieval quality:
exercises.sentence: HyDE improves search because answers match other answers better than questions do.
exercises.explain: Traditional retrieval matches questions to answers, but HyDE leverages the fact that answers match other answers much more closely in vector space.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of the following represents a hypothetical document generated by HyDE for the query 'How does photosynthesis work?':

options.text: Search query: How does photosynthesis work in plants?
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants use sunlight to synthesize nutrients from carbon dioxide and water.
Rewrite the query: 'Explain photosynthesis' to make it sound more professional.

exercises.explain: Option B is generated by an LLM simulating a hypothetical answer (document) to a user query about photosynthesis, which is then used as the search target. Options A and C are instructions or simple query modifications.

exercises.prompt: You are building a search system for a medical database. A user asks a complex question about a symptom. How does HyDE resolve this to find the best research paper?

options.text: It generates a fake answer with an LLM, embeds it, and finds similar real documents in vector space.
It rewrites the search query into three distinct questions to search simultaneously.
It bypasses the LLM entirely and uses traditional keyword matching on synonyms.
It translates the user query into multiple languages to find foreign-language articles.

exercises.explain: HyDE works by generating a hypothetical document (an LLM-created fake answer) and using its embedding to search. This works because embeddings of two answers (the fake one and the real database one) share similar structures and vocabulary, making them match much better in vector space than a raw question-to-answer pair.
